slug: 为什么太空是黑的_如果有无限颗星星不应该到处都是光吗
title: 为什么太空是黑的——如果有无限颗星星不应该到处都是光吗

outline.concept: Olbers' Paradox: if the universe were infinite, static, and eternally old, every line of sight would eventually hit a star — the night sky should be wall-to-wall starlight, as bright as the surface of a sun. The darkness itself is the anomaly that needs explaining.
outline.query: 夜空 星星 无数 为什么不亮 满天

outline.concept: The universe has a finite age (~13.8 billion years). Light has a speed limit. So there exists a cosmic horizon — beyond it, starlight simply hasn't had enough time to reach your eye yet. You're only seeing a bubble, not the whole thing.
outline.query: 远方 看不到 等待 距离 光到达

outline.concept: The universe is expanding, which stretches photon wavelengths en route — redshift. Distant galaxies' visible light gets dragged into infrared and radio bands that your eye can't register. The light arrives; you just can't see it.
outline.query: 红色 偏移 颜色变化 远处 变暗 波长

outline.concept: Darkness is not emptiness — it's the fingerprint of a universe that had a beginning and is still stretching. If the sky were uniformly bright, that would imply an eternal, static cosmos. The black between stars is the data.
outline.query: 黑暗 证据 宇宙 起源 开始 有起点
